channel.title: Neferine - Blog
channel.description: Developer based in the Philippines building web, mobile, and desktop applications with a deep love for the backend.

item.title: Why I build everything in Rust
item.description:
Performance, safety, and the satisfaction of a compiler that catches your mistakes before users ever see them. These are the reasons I've gravitated toward Rust for nearly everything I build.

The Compiler is Your Best Friend

In most languages, you discover bugs at runtime—often in production. With Rust, the compiler catches entire classes of bugs before your code even runs. Null pointer exceptions? Gone. Data races? Eliminated. Memory leaks? Practically impossible.

This isn't just about safety—it's about confidence. When the compiler says your code is correct, you can deploy with certainty. That peace of mind is invaluable when you're building systems that need to be reliable.

Performance Without Sacrifice

Rust gives you C-level performance without the memory management nightmare. Zero-cost abstractions mean you get high-level constructs that compile down to efficient machine code. No garbage collector pauses, no runtime overhead—just fast, predictable execution.

For web backends, this means handling more requests with fewer resources. For WebAssembly, it means smaller binaries and faster load times. For CLI tools, it means instant startup and responsive user experience.

The Ecosystem is Thriving

When I first started with Rust, the ecosystem was nascent. Today, it's robust and growing rapidly. From web frameworks like Axum and Actix to embedded systems with embedded-hal, there's a crate for practically everything you need.

The community is thoughtful and pragmatic. Documentation is excellent. Tooling like Cargo makes dependency management a breeze. It's a mature ecosystem that respects your time and intelligence.

It's Just Better Engineering

At the end of the day, Rust encourages better software design. The borrow checker forces you to think about data ownership and lifetimes. The type system pushes you toward more explicit, clearer code. Error handling with Result and Option makes impossible states unrepresentable.

These aren't limitations—they're guardrails that lead to more maintainable, more reliable software. And that's what engineering is all about: building things that work correctly and continue to work over time.

item.title: WebAssembly is not a JavaScript replacement
item.description:
A nuanced look at what WASM actually is, where it shines, and why the two work better together than apart.

The Misconception

Every few months, I see another article declaring "WebAssembly will replace JavaScript." It's an appealing narrative—compiled code running in the browser at near-native speeds. But this misses the fundamental point of WebAssembly.

WebAssembly was never designed to replace JavaScript. It was designed to complement it. Think of it less as a replacement and more as a specialized tool that extends what's possible in the browser.

What WebAssembly Actually Is

WebAssembly is a compilation target. It's a binary instruction format that runs alongside JavaScript in the browser, not instead of it. The key insight is that WASM and JavaScript can call each other seamlessly.

JavaScript handles the DOM, user interactions, and application logic. WebAssembly handles the heavy lifting—computationally intensive tasks, performance-critical algorithms, and codebases written in languages like Rust, C++, or Go.

Where WASM Shines

The sweet spot for WebAssembly isn't replacing everyday JavaScript. It's in scenarios where JavaScript's performance limitations become apparent:

Image and video processing: Manipulating pixels at scale, applying filters, encoding/decoding media files. These operations are orders of magnitude faster in WASM.

Scientific computing: Complex mathematical operations, data analysis, simulations. Think running Python's NumPy or R's statistical functions directly in the browser.

Game engines: Physics calculations, rendering pipelines, AI behavior. Games like Figma's design tool or AutoCAD's web version demonstrate this perfectly.

Cryptography and security: Hashing, encryption, blockchain operations. These benefit from WASM's predictable performance and memory safety.

The JavaScript Partnership

The most successful WASM applications don't eliminate JavaScript—they integrate with it. JavaScript sets up the UI, handles user events, manages application state. WASM modules are imported and called like any other JavaScript function.

This partnership is powerful. JavaScript's flexibility and ecosystem combined with WASM's performance creates possibilities that neither technology could achieve alone.

The Developer Experience

From a developer perspective, WebAssembly opens up the entire ecosystem of compiled languages to web development. Want to use Rust's memory safety? C++'s performance? Go's concurrency? Now you can, without leaving the browser.

Tools like wasm-bindgen make this integration seamless. You write Rust code, compile it to WASM, and generate JavaScript bindings automatically. The result feels natural—like calling any other JavaScript function.

The Future is Cooperative

Rather than replacing JavaScript, WebAssembly is expanding what's possible on the web. The future isn't WASM or JavaScript—it's WASM and JavaScript.

As browsers add more WebAssembly features (direct DOM access, threading, garbage collection), the line between the two will blur further. But the fundamental relationship will remain: JavaScript as the orchestrator, WebAssembly as the specialist.

That's not a replacement story. That's a collaboration story—and it's far more exciting.

item.title: Building a text editor from scratch
item.description:
Notes on gap buffers, rope data structures, and the surprising complexity of something that looks simple.

It Starts Simple

A text editor seems straightforward: display text, let users type, save the result. How hard could it be? The answer, as I discovered, is "surprisingly complex."

The naive approach is just storing text in a string. Insert at cursor position, delete from cursor position. This works for tiny files, but with a 100MB document, every insertion becomes an O(n) operation. The editor freezes.

The Gap Buffer Solution

The first optimization most editors discover is the gap buffer. Instead of one string, you maintain a buffer with a gap at the cursor position. Insertions and deletions become O(1) operations—just move the gap boundaries.

But the gap buffer has its own problems. Large cursor jumps mean moving huge amounts of data to relocate the gap. Multiple cursors? Multiple gaps? The simple solution gets complicated quickly.

Enter the Rope

This is where rope data structures come in. A rope is essentially a binary tree where each leaf contains a chunk of text. Instead of moving text around, you're just rearranging pointers.

Insertions become tree insertions. Deletions become tree deletions. The text itself never moves—only the structure changes. For large files, this is transformative.

But ropes bring their own complexity. Tree rebalancing, memory management, maintaining line numbers for display. Each optimization reveals new challenges.

Beyond the Data Structure

Once you solve the core data structure problem, you discover the real complexity is in everything else:

Syntax highlighting: Not just coloring keywords, but handling nested languages, performance optimizations, and incremental parsing.

Undo/redo: Seems simple until you need to handle complex operations, memory usage, and collaborative editing.

File handling: Large files, encoding detection, atomic saves, file watching. Each edge case matters.

Performance: Virtual scrolling for huge files, background parsing, responsive UI during heavy operations.

The Rendering Challenge

Displaying text efficiently is its own discipline. You can't just render the entire file—you need viewports, line wrapping calculations, font metrics, and smooth scrolling.

Modern editors use techniques like texture atlases for glyphs, GPU acceleration for rendering, and sophisticated caching strategies. The display layer can be as complex as the data structure layer.

The WebAssembly Advantage

This is where WebAssembly changes the game. Building a performant text editor in JavaScript hits limitations quickly. Garbage collection pauses, memory overhead, and single-threaded performance become bottlenecks.

With WASM, you get predictable performance, manual memory management, and multithreading capabilities. A rope implementation in Rust can be significantly faster and more memory-efficient than its JavaScript counterpart.

It's a deceptively simple problem that reveals layers of complexity. Each solution opens new challenges. Each optimization reveals new edge cases.

But that's what makes it fascinating. The text editor is a microcosm of software engineering—simple on the surface, endlessly complex underneath.
